description: B's test utterances (Block B2 step 7, extended B3 step 12). 16 utterances against fixtures/graph-tcp.json, covering: clean correct, vague hand-wave, confidently wrong, multi-concept ramble, off-topic derail, a dodged-with-context case, and a dodged-with-long-context case that exercises the CP3 6-turn transcript window trim. Also A's policy test inputs and the seeds for the Block B4 adversarial scripts (rambler / confident-wrong / derailer). Optional `transcript` gives prior turns for utterances that need context (e.g. dodging a direct question).

utterances.text: The congestion window is a sender-side limit on how much unacknowledged data can be in flight at once, and it gets adjusted up or down based on ACKs and loss.
If the sender gets three duplicate ACKs for the same segment, it retransmits that segment immediately instead of waiting for the retransmission timer to expire.
TCP increases its window additively each round trip when things are going fine, but cuts it multiplicatively, like halving it, the moment it sees a loss - that's AIMD.
it like... speeds up until it doesn't, you know?
there's a threshold thing that changes how it grows, somehow
after a loss it kind of just... recovers and keeps going I think
Slow start doesn't actually grow the window at all - it keeps the window flat at one segment the whole time, right up until ssthresh is hit, and only then does it start increasing.
Packet loss happens when the receiver's buffer is full, so it just refuses new packets - that's literally what tells the sender to slow down.
Fast recovery means going all the way back to slow start and rebuilding the window from scratch after any packet loss.

So it starts slow then speeds up and also sometimes packets get lost and it has to like, resend them, and there's a threshold thing too I think, and then it goes back to increasing but slower after that.
The window doubles each round trip, and if three duplicate acks show up it resends without waiting, but if a timeout happens instead the whole window just resets and it starts over from slow start.

The window is basically how much data you can have out there unacknowledged, and when you lose a packet that's the signal something's wrong, so you cut back hard but then creep back up slowly, additive-increase-multiplicative-decrease or whatever it's called.
Wait, is this related to how Wi-Fi works? My Wi-Fi has been so bad lately.
Can we talk about something else, this is kind of boring.

transcript.text: So after slow start, the growth changes to something else.
wait, changes how exactly? what makes it switch?

utterances.text: I mean, it just switches, that's kinda just how TCP works, anyway what else do you want to know?

transcript.text: Slow start doubles the window each round trip until something happens.
Until ssthresh or a loss happens.
okay. and totally separate question - does congestion avoidance use the same doubling?
No, it's additive, one segment per round trip roughly.
makes sense. anyway, going back to what tells the sender to slow down in the first place -
the receiver's ack window shrinks I think
no wait, I mean what actually signals congestion happened at all?

utterances.text: I mean it's kind of just detected, moving on, do you want to hear about ssthresh instead?

With SACK, the receiver tells the sender about every segment that actually arrived, not just the cumulative ack point, so the sender can retransmit only the holes instead of everything after the first loss.
ECN lets routers mark packets when they're getting congested instead of dropping them, and the endpoints see that mark and cut their rate before a loss happens.
ssthresh is the slow-start threshold — once the congestion window reaches it, TCP leaves slow start and grows much more carefully in congestion avoidance.
Congestion is detected either by a retransmission timeout or by getting three duplicate ACKs; either one means the network is overloaded and the sender should cut back.
After a timeout loss, TCP Tahoe drops the window back and restarts slow start from one segment, rebuilding carefully.
Fast recovery cuts the window in half after triple-duplicate-ACK loss but keeps sending from the new window instead of falling all the way back into slow start.
The whole point of congestion control is to keep senders from injecting so much data that router buffers overflow and the network collapses.
SACK is like... telling the other side which bits you got, somehow, so they don't resend everything.
ECN is when the network kind of warns you before packets actually die I think.
